content.full_text:
(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
Mr. Loren V. Smith 
California Advocates, Inc. 
Park Executive Building 
925 L Street, Suite 380 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
E~ecutive/Legol 
322-5901 
March 6, 1985 
Enforcement 
•• 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6441 
322-64« 
RE: 
FPPC Advice Letter A-85-055 
Thank you for your letter of February 26, 1985, requesting an 
informal opinion concerning your activities as a registered 
lobbyist. 
The facts as I understand them are as follows: 
You are a 
registered lobbyist representing the California Automobile 
Dealers Association. 
At times you are requested to appear before 
the New Motor Vehicle Board. 
This Board rules on issues 
involving automobile dealers, manufacturers and the Department of 
Motor Vehicles. 
A member of this Board is an automobile dealer 
and is considering hiring you as a lobbyist to work on issues 
separate and apart from his activities as an automobile dealer or 
as a Board member. 
You asked, based on the above facts, if you 
would be under any special regulatory constraints or if there 
might be a real or implied conflict in this working relationship. 
There is nothing contained in the Political Reform Act 
prohibiting this type of a working relationship. 
During the 
period of time you are employed by the Board member, you may not 
provide any discounts to the Board member or provide anything 
else of value which would violate Government Code Section 86203, 
the gift prohibition. 
Sincerely, 
~~ 
Political Reform Consultant 
MAK:bb 


011 
~ ,I 
C::::V9 U 
Park Executive Bldg., 925 L Strftet"Suite 380, Sacramento, CA 95814 (916) 441-5050 
Itn/~I 
"' ",I 
February 26, 1985 
Mary Ann Kvasager 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
11 00 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mary Ann: 
This letter is a request for an informal opinion from the FPPC staff 
regarding the following factual situation: 
I am a registered lobbyist representing, among others. the California 
Automobile Dealers Association. 
Occasionally, I am requested to 
appear before the New Motor Vehicle Board, a body which rules on issues 
involving dealers, manufacturers and the Department of Motor Vehicles. 
A dealer member of that Board is considering hiring me as an advocate 
on issues separate and apart from his activities as a dealer or board 
member. 
Since he is a "reportable person" under FPPC rules and regu-
lations, am I under any special regulatory constraints in representing 
him? 
If you feel there is no real or implied conflict in this situation, please 
outline my reporting requirements for any activities or fees received. 
V. Smith 
LVSjvp

sections.question: I am a registered lobbyist representing, among others, the California Automobile Dealers Association. Occasionally, I am requested to appear before the New Motor Vehicle Board, a body which rules on issues involving dealers, manufacturers and the Department of Motor Vehicles. A dealer member of that Board is considering hiring me as an advocate on issues separate and apart from his activities as a dealer or board member. Since he is a "reportable person" under FPPC rules and regulations, am I under any special regulatory constraints in representing him? If you feel there is no real or implied conflict in this situation, please outline my reporting requirements for any activities or fees received.
sections.conclusion: There is nothing contained in the Political Reform Act prohibiting this type of a working relationship. During the period of time you are employed by the Board member, you may not provide any discounts to the Board member or provide anything else of value which would violate Government Code Section 86203, the gift prohibition.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a registered lobbyist may represent a Board member who is also an automobile dealer on issues separate from the Board member's official duties, and if so, what regulatory constraints or reporting requirements apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the lobbyist from representing the Board member on separate issues. The only constraint is that the lobbyist cannot provide gifts or discounts to the Board member that would violate Government Code Section 86203.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated February 26, 1985) and FPPC response (dated March 6, 1985). Extracted question and conclusion from the respective sections. FPPC response is clear and definitive. Document ID A-85-055 confirms this is an advice letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I am a registered lobbyist representing, among others, the California Automobile Dealers Association. Occasionally, I am requested to appear before the New Motor Vehicle Board, a body which rules on issues involving dealers, manufacturers and the Department of Motor Vehicles. A dealer member of that Board is considering hiring me as an advocate on issues separate and apart from his activities as a dealer or board member. Since he is a "reportable person" under FPPC rules and regulations, am I under any special regulatory constraints in representing him? If you feel there is no real or implied conflict in this situation, please outline my reporting requirements for any activities or fees received.

CONCLUSION: There is nothing contained in the Political Reform Act prohibiting this type of a working relationship. During the period of time you are employed by the Board member, you may not provide any discounts to the Board member or provide anything else of value which would violate Government Code Section 86203, the gift prohibition.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a registered lobbyist's inquiry about representing an automobile dealer who is also a member of the New Motor Vehicle Board on issues separate from the Board member's official duties. The FPPC concludes that such representation is permissible under the Political Reform Act, provided the lobbyist does not violate gift prohibition laws.
